skill.name: pyopenms
skill.description: Guides pyOpenMS workflows for computational mass spectrometry, including LC-MS/MS file handling, signal processing, feature detection, peptide and protein identification, quantification, and metabolomics analysis.
skill.summary: Build and review pyOpenMS mass spectrometry workflows for proteomics and metabolomics data.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, weak cryptography, sensitive key, and reconnaissance patterns. Manual review found these are false positives from Markdown code fences, scientific terminology, parameter keys, file metadata examples, and documentation URLs. No prompt injection, credential access, exfiltration, or malicious execution intent was found; the remaining real concern is normal local filesystem use for mass spectrometry files.

low_findings.title: Static external command findings are Markdown examples
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown fenced code blocks and ordinary Python examples. SKILL.md includes an installation command and Python snippets, while reference files contain pyOpenMS examples. These blocks are instructional text, not hidden executable skill code. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.96. Confidence reasoning: The reviewed lines show Markdown fences and Python examples with no dynamic command construction or shell execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show Markdown fences and Python examples with no dynamic command construction or shell execution path.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are documentation references
low_findings.description: The network findings in SKILL.md point to pyOpenMS documentation, OpenMS documentation, and the OpenMS GitHub repository. I did not find fetch, curl, webhook, upload, or other code that sends local data to these URLs. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.98. Confidence reasoning: The URLs are listed under Resources as human-readable documentation links and are not used by executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are listed under Resources as human-readable documentation links and are not used by executable code.

low_findings.title: Sensitive key and weak crypto flags are scientific context false positives
low_findings.description: The sensitive and weak cryptography detections map to terms such as params.keys(), headers.keys(), file descriptions, protein sequences, FDR, and feature identifiers. These are mass spectrometry data structures and biological analysis examples, not certificate handling, credential access, or cryptographic algorithms. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.94. Confidence reasoning: The sampled flagged lines are scientific metadata and Python dictionary or parameter access patterns, with no secret material or crypto API calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled flagged lines are scientific metadata and Python dictionary or parameter access patterns, with no secret material or crypto API calls.

low_findings.title: Legitimate local file reads and writes are central to the skill
low_findings.description: The skill guides users to load and store mzML, idXML, featureXML, consensusXML, mzTab, CSV, and related files. This is expected for mass spectrometry analysis, but users should verify paths and keep raw files separate from outputs. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE as filesystem behavior, but LOW severity due legitimate and user-directed context. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: Multiple examples explicitly call pyOpenMS load and store methods on local user-provided scientific files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples explicitly call pyOpenMS load and store methods on local user-provided scientific files.

content.value_statement: Mass spectrometry workflows require many specialized file formats, algorithms, and quality controls. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan and explain pyOpenMS workflows for proteomics and metabolomics data.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to load, inspect, and convert mzML, mzXML, idXML, featureXML, consensusXML, mzTab, FASTA, and related files.
Guides signal processing steps such as smoothing, filtering, centroiding, normalization, and peak picking.
Describes feature detection, retention time alignment, feature linking, and consensus map workflows.
Supports peptide and protein identification workflows, including search result loading, FDR filtering, and protein inference.
Covers metabolomics preprocessing, feature table creation, database annotation concepts, and export formats.
Shows how pyOpenMS objects such as MSExperiment, MSSpectrum, FeatureMap, and ConsensusMap are used.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, but it does not include a runnable pipeline package or test dataset.
Users must install pyOpenMS and any external search engines separately in their own environment.
Large LC-MS/MS files may require streaming, indexed access, or hardware planning outside the skill.
Scientific conclusions still require domain review, experimental metadata, and validation against known controls.

use_cases.title: Inspect raw MS data
use_cases.description: Load mzML files, summarize spectra, review retention times, and identify the next preprocessing step.

use_cases.title: Build feature detection workflows
use_cases.description: Plan feature detection, alignment, linking, and consensus export for multiple LC-MS samples.

use_cases.title: Prepare metabolomics tables
use_cases.description: Convert consensus features into tabular outputs for statistical analysis and downstream annotation.

prompt_templates.title: Start with a file overview
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how I should inspect an mzML file with pyOpenMS before running feature detection. Include the key objects and quality checks.

prompt_templates.title: Choose preprocessing steps
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a pyOpenMS preprocessing workflow for centroided LC-MS/MS data. I need smoothing, filtering, and basic quality control.

prompt_templates.title: Plan multi-sample quantification
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pyOpenMS workflow for three samples that detects features, aligns retention times, links features, and exports a consensus table.

prompt_templates.title: Review an identification pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my peptide identification plan for pyOpenMS. Check file formats, FDR filtering, protein inference, and places where metadata could be lost.

output_examples.input: I have one mzML file and need to understand its contents.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step inspection plan covering file loading, spectrum counts, MS levels, retention time ranges, peak counts, and early quality checks.

output_examples.input: I need to compare features across three LC-MS samples.
output_examples.output: A workflow outline for per-file feature detection, retention time alignment, feature linking, consensus map creation, and table export.

output_examples.input: My peptide IDs need filtering before protein inference.
output_examples.output: A review checklist covering idXML loading, score direction, q-value filtering, decoy strategy, protein inference, and reportable assumptions.

best_practices: Keep original raw files unchanged and write processed outputs to a separate directory.
Record parameter values, file names, pyOpenMS version, and search engine settings with every analysis.
Use small representative files to validate workflow logic before processing full experiments.
anti_patterns: Do not run feature detection without checking whether spectra are profile or centroided data.
Do not compare sample intensities before alignment, linking, and missing-value handling are planned.
Do not treat example thresholds as final scientific settings without dataset-specific validation.

faq.question: Does this skill install pyOpenMS automatically?
faq.answer: No. It explains installation and workflow patterns, but users manage packages in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it process raw vendor files directly?
faq.answer: The referenced workflows focus on open formats such as mzML. Vendor conversion may require separate tools.

faq.question: Does it replace OpenMS documentation?
faq.answer: No. It summarizes common workflows and object usage, while official documentation remains the source for full API details.

faq.question: Can it help with both proteomics and metabolomics?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for protein identification workflows and untargeted metabolomics preprocessing.

faq.question: Can it validate scientific results?
faq.answer: No. It can suggest checks, but final interpretation requires experimental controls and expert review.

faq.question: What files does it mention reading or writing?
faq.answer: It covers common MS formats such as mzML, idXML, featureXML, consensusXML, FASTA, mzTab, and related OpenMS files.
